claims.claim_text: SSH (Secure Shell) is a protocol for secure remote login and other secure network services, and terminal access over SSH is a standard way to manage remote systems.
claims.span_hint: section: Why Do Engineers Use Terminals? / 3. Remote Access
claims.rationale: RFC 4251 is the authoritative standards-track source for SSH as secure remote login.

claims.claim_text: In the February 28, 2017 Amazon S3 disruption in us-east-1, an authorized operator entered one input incorrectly, removing more servers than intended; AWS later added safeguards to reduce the chance of a repeat.
claims.span_hint: section: Why Do Engineers Use Terminals? / A Documented Operational Mistake
claims.rationale: AWS's own postmortem directly documents the incorrect command input, the broader impact, and the follow-up safeguards.

claims.claim_text: The first computers had no screens at all.
claims.anchor_text: The first computers had no screens at all.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many early computers had no interactive screens at all.
claims.rationale: The absolute phrasing is broader than the supporting history and should be narrowed to a more defensible historical statement.

claims.claim_text: Early programmers used punched cards, literal cardboard cards with holes, to provide programs and data to computers.
claims.rationale: The punched-card history page directly supports the description of cards with holes being used for computing input.

claims.claim_text: Graphical user interfaces emerged in 1970s systems and became mainstream in the 1980s with personal computers such as the Apple Macintosh.
claims.rationale: The GUI history page covers 1970s Xerox-era development and the Macintosh-era popularization in the 1980s.

claims.claim_text: In the 1960s-70s, a terminal was a separate hardware device connected to a larger computer, and today's terminal applications are called terminal emulators because they emulate those earlier terminals.
claims.rationale: The computer terminal history page describes hardware terminals and the later role of terminal emulators.

claims.claim_text: Windows Terminal is Microsoft's terminal application for shells such as PowerShell, Command Prompt, and bash via WSL.
claims.span_hint: section: Opening Your Terminal / Windows / Option B
claims.rationale: Microsoft's Windows Terminal overview directly describes the product and the shells it hosts.

claims.claim_text: Microsoft documents `wsl --install` as the one-command way to install WSL, with Ubuntu installed by default and a restart required.
claims.span_hint: section: Opening Your Terminal / Windows / Option C
claims.rationale: The official WSL install guide matches the exact command and restart flow shown in the module.

claims.claim_text: GitLab's January 31, 2017 outage involved accidental removal of data from the primary database, causing a major outage and some unrecoverable production data loss.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes in Production / first table row
claims.rationale: GitLab's published postmortem directly states the accidental data removal, outage, and unrecoverable data loss.

further_reading.title: Command line crash course
further_reading.why_relevant: It is a beginner-friendly follow-up that explains what the terminal is and how to use a few basic commands.

further_reading.title: RFC 4251: The Secure Shell (SSH) Protocol Architecture
further_reading.why_relevant: It gives the authoritative definition of SSH, which is the standard remote-access protocol mentioned in the module.

further_reading.title: Install WSL
further_reading.why_relevant: It is the official next-step guide for Windows learners who want a Linux-compatible terminal environment.

notes: The module already contains inline links for OpenSSH, AWS, and GitLab; RFC 4251 can cover the SSH standards claim on an allowlisted host, while the main remaining citation work is the historical material in the Did You Know section and the Windows-specific setup claims.
